feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: A simple, whitespace theme for academics. Based on [*folio](https://github.com/bogoli/-folio) design.

entry.title: Cluster-Based Permutation Test
entry.content:
Imagine you’re a researcher who has just collected some amazing data, maybe EEG signals over 1000 time points or fMRI data across thousands of brain voxels. You want to see where and when your experimental condition differs from a control condition.
The immediate temptation is to run a t-test at every single time point or voxel. This leads directly to the multiple comparisons problem. Introduce this problem with a simple analogy.

  • The Jelly Bean Test: If you test one jelly bean for being poisonous, a p-value of 0.05 is fine. But if you test 1000 jelly beans, you’re almost guaranteed to find a “significant” one just by random chance, even if none are actually poisonous. This is what happens when you test thousands of data points.

Conclude the intro by positioning permutation test, is a non parametric method to deal with Multiple Comparison Problem(MCP), and is pretty common use in M/EEG data(multiple channel, multiple time and multiple frequency), because it will easily cause type I error which is wrongly reject null hypothesis.

How It Works: A Step-by-Step Breakdown

We have a dataset from 3 people, with recordings taken before (pre) and after (post) a meditation session. For each person, we measured the power spectrum across 5 electrode channels and 10 frequency bins.

This gives us a 5x10 grid of data for each person. When we calculate the pre-post difference, we have 50 statistical tests we could run (5 channels * 10 frequencies). If we run 50 separate t-tests, we run into the multiple comparisons problem. We are likely to find “significant” results just by random chance.

This is where the cluster-based permutation test comes in. It’s designed to solve this exact problem by looking for patterns—or clusters—in the data, rather than individual points.

Step 1: Calculate Raw Statistics

First, you do exactly what you initially thought of: perform a separate statistical test (like a t-test) for every single data point (e.g., each time point and sensor). This gives you a map of raw t-values.

Step 2: Apply a Significance Threshold

Next, we need to decide which t-values are “interesting.” We do this by setting a cluster-entry threshold. This is usually an uncorrected p-value, like p<0.05. Any t-value that is more extreme than this threshold is considered a “candidate” to be part of a cluster.

All the points that don’t pass this threshold are discarded. This helps us focus only on the points showing a relatively strong effect.

Step 3: Find Clusters of Adjacent Points

Now, we look at our thresholded map and group together any candidate points that are adjacent to each other. Adjacency can be defined in different ways, but for our 2D grid, we can say points are adjacent if they touch at the sides (not just diagonally).
This process results in one or more “clusters”—islands of significant effects.
In our example, we can see two distinct clusters emerge: a larger, positive cluster (in red) and a smaller, negative one (in blue). These are circled to make them clear.

Figure for step1 to step 3, with black circled the MCP matrix with p-val < 0.05

Step 4: Calculate Cluster “Mass”

The “size” of each cluster isn’t just how many points it contains; it’s about the total strength of the effect within it. We calculate this by summing up all the individual t-values of the points inside each cluster. This sum is called the cluster mass or cluster-level statistic.\ We then take the largest of these (in absolute value) as our single, most important number from our real data. \Here, we’ve labeled our one clusters with their calculated mass. The red cluster has a mass of let’s say 30 (t-val). This is our observed_max_cluster_mass.

Step 5: Replacement and clustering statistics

So, is a cluster mass of 30 big? Or could we get a cluster that large just by chance?
To find out, we create a null distribution. We pretend the null hypothesis is true (that meditation has no effect). We do this by randomly shuffling our data. For each of our 3 subjects, we randomly either keep their “pre” and “post” labels as they are, or we swap them.

With this randomly shuffled data, we repeat the entire process:

  • Calculate new t-values.

  • Threshold them.

  • Find clusters.

  • Find the maximum cluster mass on the shuffled data.

We do this over and over again—typically 1,000 or more times. This gives us 1,000+ “maximum cluster mass” values that could have been found under the null hypothesis.
Plotting all those random maximum cluster values gives us a histogram—our null distribution. We can then see where our actual observed value falls on this distribution. Is it in the main body of the distribution, or is it an outlier?

Step 6: Calculate the Final p-value

The final step is wonderfully simple. We just calculate the proportion of random shuffles that resulted in a maximum cluster mass greater than or equal to our observed one.

If we ran 1,000 permutations and only 20 of them produced a max cluster mass of 30 or more, our p-value would be 20 / 1000 = 0.02.

This p-value applies to the entire cluster.

Figure for step1 to step 3, with black circled the MCP matrix with p-val < 0.05

This final image shows our null distribution, with the area corresponding to the p-value shaded in. This shaded area represents the “suprise factor”—how unlikely our result was under the null hypothesis.

Conclusion: What It All Means

So, what can we conclude from our analysis?

With a final cluster-p-value of 0.02, we can confidently say that the positive cluster we observed is statistically significant. Our interpretation would be:

“We found a significant positive cluster (p=0.02) indicating that meditation led to an increase in power. This effect was most prominent in the frontal an central channels and across the 5Hz to 8Hz frequency range.”

Crucially, we cannot pick a single point within that cluster (e.g., Cz, 7Hz) and say it is significant. The test only gives us confidence in the cluster as a whole. This is the trade-off: we lose spatial/focal precision but gain a huge amount of statistical power and solve the multiple comparisons problem.

entry.title: Motor Imagery BCI Application – Exploring Common Spatial Pattern (CSP) Mathematical Understanding

link.title: Motor Imagery BCI Application – Exploring Common Spatial Pattern (CSP) Mathematical Understanding

entry.content:
Common Spatial Pattern (CSP) is a widely used spatial filtering algorithm in the field of brain-computer interfaces (BCIs), particularly effective for classifying motor imagery paradigms. It is considered one of the benchmark algorithms in this domain.

In BCI classification pipelines, algorithms typically consist of three key components: feature generation, feature selection, and classification.

In recent years, the development of CSP and its variants has slowed down, seemingly reaching a plateau with few major breakthroughs. Nevertheless, the core ideas behind CSP continue to offer valuable insights for the design of BCI algorithms.

This article begins with a derivation of the original CSP algorithm, with a particular focus on how spatial features are generated from EEG data. It aims to elucidate the underlying mathematical principles.

Spatial filters Generating

Using the CSP algorithm by linearly transforming the EEG meassurement using:

\[X_{csp}^{(i)} = W^TX^{(i)}\]

$X^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_c \times n_t}$ is the single-trial EEG measurement from ith trial; $X_{csp}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_c \times n_t}$ denotes $X^{(i)}$ after spatial filtering, $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n_c \times n_c}$ denotes the CSP projection matrix.

How to get the $W$, the key idea is to after the transformation matrix $W$ to yield the feature whose variances are optimal for discriminating 2 classes of EEG measurements. Here is the derivation of how to get $W$ (See figure below)

CSP FILTERS MAXIMIZE THE VARIANCE OF THE SPATIALLY FILTERED SIGNAL UNDER ONE CONDITION WHILE MINIMIZING IT FOR THE OTHER CONDITION.
  1. Compute covariance of $X^{(i)}_1$ and $X^{(i)}_2$ (1 and 2 denotes label) which is $C_1^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_c \times n_c}$, $C_2^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_c \times n_c}$
  2. The take average from all trials for the label =1 and 2, then denotes them as $\bar{C_1}$ and $\bar{C_2}$

  3. Since we want to yield variances to discriminating 2 classes, which equalivalent to each channel data after the transform is irrelevant.

    \[\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \Lambda_{\text{csp}} &= X_{\text{csp}} X_{\text{csp}}^T \\ &= (W^T X)(W^T X)^T \\ &= W^T X X^T W \\ &= W^T C W \end{aligned} \tag{1} \end{equation}\]

    $\Lambda_{csp}$ should be a dialognal matrix. (covariance of each channel after projection is = 0) and since we want to discriminating two classes, we want one class variance is larger and the other one smaller. So that we can get a relationship which $\Lambda_1 + \Lambda_2 = I$, $(\lambda_1 = 1- \lambda_2)$ here $\Lambda_1$ and $\Lambda_2$ correspond to eignvalue decompastion of the $X_{csp}X_{csp}^T$ of label 1 and 2 data.

  4. Now we can get the is trivial to see that:

    \[\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} I &= \Lambda_{1}+\Lambda_{2}\\ &= X_{scp1}X_{scp1}^T + X_{scp2}X_{scp2}^T \\&= W^TX_1X_1^TW+ W^TX_2X_2^TW \\&= W^T\bar{C_1}W + W^T\bar{C_2}W \\ & = W^T(\bar{C_1}+\bar{C_2})W\\ &=W^T\bar{C}_cW \end{aligned} \tag{2} \end{equation}\]

    and we can get $W = \bar{C}_cW$

  5. Plugin $W = \bar{C}_cW$ into equation (1), we can get the equation below:

    \[\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} W^T\bar{C_1}W &= \Lambda_1\\ \bar{C_1}W &= W\Lambda_1 \\ \bar{C_1}W &= \bar{C}_cW\Lambda_1 \quad &\text{(Plugin $W = \bar{C}_cW$)} \\ \bar{C_1}W &= (\bar{C}_1+\bar{C}_2)W\Lambda_1 \quad &\text{($\bar{C}_c=\bar{C}_1+\bar{C}_2$)} \\ \end{aligned} \tag{3} \end{equation}\]

    This equation (3) can be look as $Ax = \lambda Bx$. This type of formulation is known as a generalized eigenvalue problem. Solving generalized eigenvalue problems forms the foundation of traditional spatial filtering methods in the brain-computer interface (BCI) field. Many algorithms in this area can be reformulated into this generalized eigenvalue structure.

    Understand this equation (3) more intuitively, consider this $\Lambda_1$, is the eigenvalue of matrix $(\bar{C}_c)^{-1}\bar{C}_1$ which is the ratio between combine covariance matrix of label 1 and 2 data. The larger the eigenvalue the larger variance of label 1 data and smaller the variance of label 2 data, vice versa.

Selection of spatial filters

Now is easy to compute the $W$, projection matrix/spatial filter. In Matlab is simply

W = eig(C_1,C_1+C_2)

In python is

W = numpy.linalg.eig(C_1,C_1+C_2)

This is the math formulation of the spital filter, and it’s not end it yet - hang on!

The motivation behind using CSP is to extract features that make it easier to distinguish between two classes. However, generating too many features can be detrimental to model training, as it increases the risk of overfitting. In addition, irrelevant or noisy features can negatively affect the accuracy of the classifier.

To address this, feature pruning becomes essential. Since CSP relies on eigenvalue decomposition, it naturally provides a mechanism for feature selection. When the eigenvalues are sorted in descending order, they guide us in identifying the most informative features. Specifically, the largest and smallest eigenvalues correspond to directions where the variance is maximized for one class and minimized for the other.

By selecting the eigenvectors associated with the largest and smallest eigenvalues, we can construct a compressed projection matrix $ W $ that retains the most discriminative spatial features while reducing the risk of overfitting. After applying this projection, we obtain a filtered feature matrix that is well-suited for classification tasks.

At present, the value of $ m $ in CSP algorithms is typically chosen based on empirical heuristics—usually selecting 2 to 4 components.

The final feature is getting from the equation (4) below. Basically do a normalization.

\[\begin{equation} \mathbf{v}_{b,i} = \log \left( \frac{ \operatorname{diag}\left( \widetilde{\mathbf{W}}_b^\top \mathbf{E}_{b,i} \mathbf{E}_{b,i}^\top \widetilde{\mathbf{W}}_b \right) }{ \operatorname{tr} \left[ \widetilde{\mathbf{W}}_b^\top \mathbf{E}_{b,i} \mathbf{E}_{b,i}^\top \widetilde{\mathbf{W}}_b \right] } \right) \tag{4} \end{equation}\]

$\bar{\mathbf{W}}_b$ represents the first $m$ and the last $m$ columns of $\mathbf{W}_b$;
$\operatorname{diag}(\cdot)$ returns the diagonal elements of a square matrix;
$\operatorname{tr}[\cdot]$ returns the sum of the diagonal elements of a square matrix.

Reference

For more details, refer to the original CSP paper.

Some other useful blogs to understand 闲谈之共空间模式（Common Spatial Pattern，CSP)
entry.summary: Foundation of ML in BCI Application
